title: Argument Game Proof Theories and Dialogues

charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of argument game proof theories, winning strategies, and the generalisation to dialogues for distributed reasoning.

nodes.label:
Week 3
Argument Games
nodes.note: Week 3 introduces argument games as an alternative to computing labellings directly. Two players exchange attacking arguments to test whether a given argument is in the grounded or preferred labelling.

nodes.label:
Recap
Labellings
nodes.note: An AF labelling assigns IN, OUT, or UN to each argument. The grounded labelling is the most sceptical (smallest IN set); preferred labellings maximise the IN set.

nodes.label:
Argument
Game Idea
nodes.note: Two players (PRO and OPP) take turns playing arguments that attack the previous one. PRO defends the initial argument; OPP tries to defeat it.

nodes.label:
Grounded
Game
nodes.note: The grounded game tests membership in the grounded labelling. Its key restriction is that PRO cannot repeat any argument in a single dispute.

nodes.label:
Preferred
Game
nodes.note: The preferred game tests membership in some preferred labelling. OPP cannot repeat, and the winning strategy must contain no mutually attacking PRO arguments.

nodes.label:
Winning
Strategies
nodes.note: A winning strategy is a subtree of the game tree where every OPP node has all children, every PRO node has at least one child, and every leaf is a node where OPP is stuck.

nodes.label:
Dialogues
(LO3 non-exam)
nodes.note: Argument games generalise to multi-agent dialogues with shared locutions like claim, why, and argue. Each dialogue defines an implicit AF over its locution graph.

nodes.label:
AI Applications
(LO4 non-exam)
nodes.note: Dialogue-based argumentation supports value alignment in AI systems. SocrAPPs use Socratic dialogue to foster critical thinking in users.

nodes.label:
Rule G3
PRO no-repeat
nodes.note: In the grounded game, PRO cannot reuse an argument in the same dispute. This avoids cyclic support, matching the conservative grounded semantics.

nodes.label:
Rule P3
OPP no-repeat
nodes.note: In the preferred game, OPP cannot reuse an argument in the same dispute. This makes OPP's task harder, matching the more credulous preferred semantics.

nodes.label:
WS Condition
(no PRO conflict)
nodes.note: In a preferred winning strategy, no two PRO arguments may attack each other. This guarantees the strategy corresponds to a conflict-free set of arguments.

nodes.label:
Locutions
& Protocols
nodes.note: A locution is a formalised utterance: claim(q), why(q), or argue(X,q). A dialogue protocol specifies which locutions are legal in response to others.

nodes.label:
Soundness &
Completeness
nodes.note: Combined SC theorem: a dialogue establishes claim(q) iff q is a non-monotonic inference from all the knowledge exchanged in the dialogue.

charts.title: Grounded Game Procedure
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for testing whether an argument X0 is in the grounded labelling using the grounded argument game.

nodes.label:
Test: is X0 in
grounded labelling?
nodes.note: We are given an AF and an argument X0 and want to use the grounded game to decide whether X0 is in the grounded labelling.

nodes.label:
Build complete
grounded game tree
nodes.note: Construct the full game tree rooted at X0, where PRO and OPP alternate moves and each move must attack the previous argument.

nodes.label:
Apply G3:
PRO no-repeat
nodes.note: Prune branches where PRO would repeat an argument it already played in the same dispute. PRO is not allowed to reuse arguments.

nodes.label:
List all disputes
(root-to-leaf paths)
nodes.note: Enumerate every root-to-leaf path of the pruned game tree. Each path is a complete dispute that ends with one player being stuck.

nodes.label:
Find subtree T'
where PRO wins all
nodes.note: Look for a subtree T' that includes all children at OPP nodes and at least one child at PRO nodes, such that every leaf is a node where OPP cannot move.

nodes.label:
Such subtree
exists?
nodes.note: Check whether a winning strategy subtree was found. If yes, PRO has a winning strategy; otherwise no winning strategy exists.

nodes.label:
X0 IS in
grounded labelling
nodes.note: PRO has a winning strategy, so by the soundness/completeness theorem X0 is in the grounded labelling of the AF.

nodes.label:
X0 NOT in
grounded labelling
nodes.note: PRO has no winning strategy, so X0 is not in the grounded labelling. It is either OUT or UN under the grounded semantics.

charts.title: Preferred Game Procedure
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for testing whether an argument X0 is in some preferred labelling using the preferred argument game.

nodes.label:
Test: is X0 in
some preferred labelling?
nodes.note: We are given an AF and an argument X0 and want to use the preferred game to decide whether X0 belongs to at least one preferred labelling.

nodes.label:
Build complete
preferred game tree
nodes.note: Construct the full game tree rooted at X0, where PRO and OPP alternate moves and each move attacks the previous one.

nodes.label:
Apply P3:
OPP no-repeat
nodes.note: Prune branches where OPP would repeat an argument it already played in the same dispute. PRO is allowed to repeat in the preferred game.

nodes.label:
Find candidate
winning subtree T'
nodes.note: Look for a subtree where OPP nodes have all their children, PRO nodes have at least one child, and every leaf is a node where OPP cannot move.

nodes.label:
Check WS condition:
no PRO/PRO attacks
nodes.note: Verify that no two PRO arguments inside the candidate subtree attack each other. This ensures the strategy corresponds to a conflict-free set.

nodes.label:
X0 IS in some
preferred labelling
nodes.note: PRO has a valid winning strategy that satisfies the WS condition, so X0 belongs to at least one preferred labelling of the AF.

nodes.label:
Another candidate
subtree available?
nodes.note: If the WS condition fails, try a different candidate subtree before giving up. PRO can pick different responses at PRO nodes.

nodes.label:
X0 NOT in any
preferred labelling
nodes.note: No valid winning strategy exists, so X0 is not in any preferred labelling. It is OUT or UN under every preferred labelling.

charts.title: Grounded vs Preferred Game - Comparison
charts.description: Side-by-side classification of the two argument games and their differences.

nodes.label:
Argument
Games
nodes.note: Two-player turn-taking games used to decide whether an argument is in a target labelling. PRO defends, OPP attacks.

nodes.label:
Shared
rules
nodes.note: Both games share the basic mechanics: PRO moves first, players alternate, and each move must attack the previous one.

nodes.label:
Grounded
Game
nodes.note: Designed to detect membership in the grounded (sceptical) labelling. PRO must not repeat itself in a dispute.

nodes.label:
Preferred
Game
nodes.note: Designed to detect membership in some preferred (credulous) labelling. OPP must not repeat itself, plus a no-mutual-PRO-attack condition.

nodes.label:
G3: PRO no-
repeat
nodes.note: PRO cannot reuse an argument in the same dispute. This blocks cyclic support and matches the most conservative semantics.

nodes.label:
P3: OPP no-
repeat
nodes.note: OPP cannot reuse an argument in the same dispute. PRO is allowed to repeat, which matches the more credulous preferred semantics.

nodes.label:
WS: no two PRO
args attack
nodes.note: Extra condition on the preferred winning strategy. Ensures the set of PRO arguments is conflict-free, as required by preferred labellings.

nodes.label:
Result:
Labelling
nodes.note: If PRO has a winning strategy, the initial argument is in the corresponding labelling (grounded or some preferred).

nodes.label:
PRO/OPP
alternation
nodes.note: PRO and OPP take turns, with PRO playing the initial argument as the first move and each subsequent move attacking the previous one.

charts.title: Identifying a Winning Strategy
charts.description: Procedure for extracting a winning strategy subtree from a complete game tree.

nodes.label:
Have complete
game tree
nodes.note: We start with the full pruned game tree rooted at the initial PRO argument and want to find a subtree that constitutes a winning strategy.

nodes.label:
Identify all
leaves
nodes.note: Look at every leaf of the game tree. A leaf is a node where the player whose turn it is cannot make any further move.

nodes.label:
Each leaf:
which player stuck?
nodes.note: For each leaf, decide whether OPP or PRO is the one who has no move. PRO wins disputes ending where OPP is stuck.

nodes.label:
Mark dispute
as PRO-win
nodes.note: OPP cannot move at this leaf, so PRO wins the dispute. This dispute can be part of a winning strategy.

nodes.label:
Mark dispute
as PRO-lose
nodes.note: PRO cannot move at this leaf, so PRO loses the dispute. Any winning strategy must avoid this branch via different PRO choices.
Construct the candidate subtree by always including every child at OPP nodes (OPP can play any move) but choosing only one child at PRO nodes (PRO picks a response).

nodes.label:
All leaves of T'
are PRO-wins?
nodes.note: Check every leaf of the candidate subtree. If any leaf is a PRO-loss, this is not a winning strategy and PRO must choose differently.

nodes.label:
Winning strategy
found
nodes.note: Every dispute in the subtree is won by PRO so the subtree is a valid winning strategy. The initial argument is in the target labelling.

nodes.label:
Try different
PRO choices
nodes.note: Backtrack at PRO nodes and try other children. If no choice produces a winning subtree, no winning strategy exists.
